original_abstract:
This study evaluates the effectiveness of a Bachelor’s degree curriculum at a Moroccan university in equipping students with the 21st-century skills essential for modern employability. Adopting a mixed-methods approach, the research integrates curriculum analysis with questionnaires and semi-structured interviews involving both students and faculty members. The curriculum review assessed the integration of core competencies—such as creativity, problem-solving, and digital literacy—while the primary data gauged student perceptions of workforce readiness and explored faculty perspectives on pedagogical efficacy.

The findings reveal a significant disconnect between academic training and professional requirements. Curriculum analysis indicates that most courses provide insufficient alignment with 21st-century skills, a deficiency reflected in questionnaire results where a majority of students reported feeling unprepared for the labor market. Conversely, faculty members maintained that their instructional methods provide adequate skill development, instead attributing student unreadiness to high rates of absenteeism and a lack of academic engagement.

The study concludes that bridging this gap requires a transition toward more interactive and student-centered instruction. Furthermore, it highlights the importance of fostering metacognitive skills, such as self-motivation and personal responsibility, to cultivate resilience and lifelong learning. Ultimately, the findings underscore the urgent need for a comprehensive curriculum overhaul to better align higher education outcomes with the evolving demands of the global job market.
humanized_abstract:
Title: Assessment of Bachelor's Degree Curricula for the Development of 21st Century Competencies for Work-Future Readiness (1st Edition) Abstract: The main objective of this study is to investigate the relevance of a Bachelor's degree curriculum to the needs of the 21st century skills required by the labour market. Based on mixed-methods research methodology, this study empirically evaluated the curriculum along with the students and teaching staff views through the curriculum analysis, questionnaire and semi-structured interviews. While curriculum analysis aimed at recognizing the embedment of the required skills such as; creativity, innovation, decision-making and information and communication technologies skills, primary data collection aimed at measuring students' perceived level of their work-readiness along with teachers' views regarding the instructional strategies adopted.

The study reached the conclusion that there is a large mismatch between the knowledge and skills acquired in the educational system and the requirements of the labor market. The curriculum analysis showed that most curricula are not aligned with the needed 21st century skills, a fact that is also confirmed by the students’ opinions in the survey. The large majority of students stated that they did not acquire the needed skills to enter the labor market. On the other hand, the teachers believed that they taught the needed skills and that the reason the graduates were not ready to enter the labor market was due to high levels of absenteeism and lack of effort from the students.

THE KEY TO Bridging the Skills Gap? In order to close the skill gap, education needs to shift from a more pedagogical, towards a more interactive and student centered form. Students must be taught to develop their own metacognitive skills such as self-motivation, personal responsibility and the ability to learn from setbacks in order to be better able to cope with new, ever-changing working conditions. This calls for an overall review of the entire curriculum in higher education so that the skills learned will be in line with future needs on the world labour market.
